speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is March the 21st, 1996. My name is Linda Davidson. And we are interviewing the survivor Gerda Frieberg, Frieberg, nee Steinitz. The interview is being conducted in English and is taking place in Toronto, Canada. Today's date is March the 21st, 1996. My name is Linda Davidson. And I'm conducting an interview with the survivor Gerda Frieberg nee Steinitz. The interview is being conducted in English and is taking place in Toronto, Canada. Please state your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gerda Frieberg, F-R-I-E-B-E-R-G. My maiden name is Steinitz, S-T-E-I-N-I-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-E-R-D-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Frieberg, when were you born? What date?

speaker: interviewee
text: October 12, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Bielschowitz. Or it's now called Bielszowice, because it's now Poland. And it is now spelled B-I-E-L-S-Z-O-W-I-C-E. And it's in Upper Silesia, which is between the Polish and German border-- at the time when I was born. Now it's Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'd like to learn from you about your family. What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Josif Steinitz. My mother's name is Elfride. Her maiden name was Brenner, B-R-E-N-N-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Frieberg, what occupation did your father have before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he-- we had a general store in the town that-- where I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you, what large city was Bielszowice close to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bielszowice was a town of 25,000 people. The only Jews in town was my grandmother, my paternal grandmother, and ourselves. There were only two Jewish family in the town. And the closest city was Hindenburg, which was Germany proper. And it was about 2 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your address where you lived in Bielszowice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was number 61-- in Polish, [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to return to talk about your family. What kind of man was your father, Josif Steinitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he-- he was a businessman. But more than that, he was an athlete. He loved sports. He was racing motorcycles. That was his first love. He skied. He bicycled for great, great distances. He would love mountaineering, walking in the forests, and loved music and theater. I don't know if he loved as much business as all the other things. That was a necessity.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of father was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a wonderful father to us. I have a sister, Hana. And he never had the son that he probably wanted and kind of treated us as the two boys that he didn't have. And so we did quite adventurous things together. And he was a wonderful father. I remember when Mother was sometimes going away for a week or so with her mother for summer vacations, and he would take us everywhere. And if you went out in the evening, in the morning, we would wake up, and there would be a little box of chocolates at our bedside. So I have wonderful memories of him now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your father born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in Breslau in 1898. He was a-- when he was born, he was an orphan because his father was murdered by robbers while my grandmother was expecting the baby. And she later remarried. And he was brought up by his-- his grandparents, my great-grandparents, in Breslau. And that where he spent most of his youth till he was called to the army, to the German army, in the First World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the name of your great-grandparents in Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother's maiden name was [? Schlesinger. ?] That's my paternal grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And had the family lived in that region for many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the town where I was born and-- and lived up till the war, my great-great-grandparents built a home. And it was a station for the people that came by to-- for the horses, to rest and for water. And in-- in that town-- there was no town then. There was a forest. But they found coal. And they built a coal mine. And that's when Bielszowice grew into the town that it was when I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were virtually the first people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first people there, right. And we'd probably still be there if not for the war. I went to school there, to public school, till grade six, when I wrote an entry exam to high school, what we called 'gymnasium.' Then I went to gymnasium for two years in the neighboring town of Nowa Wies, what used to be [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to come back to that schooling in a moment. Just want to learn a little bit more about your family. So from what you've told us, your family were in Germany for generations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they-- my paternal grandmother comes from the area of Breslau. And that's-- her parents lived there. This was all Germany at one time. It changed hands in 1920 when there was a plebiscite. And people voted to-- to become Poland. They moved the border. As a matter of fact, my sister was the first baby born under Polish rule, which was 1922. But it was all German proper. My parents decided to stay. Our whole family lived in Germany because we were so close to the border, so they-- and they had already the-- the business there. And they decided to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now tell us a little bit about your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was born in the neighboring town of then [POLISH], now called Nowa Wies. And again, my maternal grandmother and my grandfather lived in that area for generations. My maternal grandfather died in 1913. He was in the Honor Guard of the Kaiser, serving in the German Army. And when he died of a heart attack at the age of 50, he was buried with military honors.

speaker: interviewer
text: In [POLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [POLISH]. The cemetery actually was in Konig City, which was a few kilometers-- you see, Upper Silesia is very heavily populated. You don't know when you leave one town and enter another one. The tragedy is that my grandfather was buried with military honors. And my grandmother, his widow, was pushed into the gas chambers of Auschwitz. My mother was born in [POLISH], went to school there. She had two brothers, Max and Fritz, and two sisters, Rose and Alice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the last name Brenner.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brenner, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of mother did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's difficult to say. She was a good mother. She was very strong. She was actually the-- the soul of the business. Very bright and devoted to Germany. She lived 18 years under Polish rule, never learned Polish. Just refused to accept that now her hometown is now Poland. And very devoted to-- to her husband. It was a very, very happy marriage-- and-- and to her children.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you have a sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close as children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had no one else, the two Jewish kids in the-- in the town. There were no-- no other Jewish children. Any cousins that we had lived a little distance away, one in Berlin, the other one in [GERMAN], which was also on the other side of the border. So we really did not have that many cousins, or we didn't see them that often. So we just relied on each other. But she's three years older. So to a certain point, we were very close. But then you know what happens. When children grow up, three years become a big difference in interest and in age and in friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what contact did you have with the extended family in Germany across the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they would come and visit for-- we always had birthday parties when the family came. We would see them in the synagogue on holidays. My maternal grandmother lived in the town where there were some little hills. And we'd go skiing there. So we'd visit every Sunday after skiing. We would visit a lot in-- in Breslau, my great-grandparents there. Because we were so isolated, my parents made it their business that we know the family, and that we visit them, and that we get together with them. I guess they wanted the Jewish input.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as a young girl growing up in Bielszowice, your friend were non-Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And the only-- well, the only Jewishness that I knew was celebrating holidays, going to the synagogue, and going for religious lessons to-- across the border to Hindenburg to the synagogue. There was the teacher that my parents hired just for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lehrer-- which means, in German, 'teacher'-- [POLISH]. And we went for private lessons once a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was Hindenburg from Bielszowice?

speaker: interviewee
text: 2 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would you travel there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Winter? Summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and when I went to high school, we walked 6 kilometers to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the first school that you attended.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first school?

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- the public school. Did it have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In-- in our country, in our-- the schools did not have names. There were four schools in the town. Ours was number four. And you would stay with the same teacher till grade five. So if my teacher happened to be an anti-Semite, then you are stuck with her. And as a matter of fact, an interesting incident that I had when I was grade six, the teacher didn't show up. And we did not have supply teachers in those days. So nobody was in the classroom. And of course, we all know what happens when children are left in a classroom without a teacher. When the principal walked in, it was pretty noisy. And he says, it's as noisy as in the Jewish prayer house. Now, I didn't know Jewish prayer houses. Only knew a synagogue. And in a German synagogue, you can hear a pin fall on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so I packed my books, and I walked out of the class. And he said, where are you going? I said, well, since you say it's as noisy as in the Jewish prayer house, then I'm probably the one that makes all the noise. He got very angry, wanted me to-- to come back. And I said, there's no way, and I left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father, of course, supported me in this. When he came running to-- in the afternoon and wanted to straighten it out, he says he never said it because he needed to use the telephone. You see, schools in the-- these towns did not have telephones. We were the only ones that had a telephone. If you wanted to call for supplies, you would come to our-- to us. So he didn't want to lose the telephone, so he came running. But my father supported me. I would-- see, at that age-- I was-- wasn't even 12 then. I was 11. I already spoke up and didn't let it sit. So I didn't endear myself to him, but I made my point.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did being Jewish mean to you at the age of 11, 12?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably not much. The only thing that I wouldn't let anyone insult the Jewish people. That's probably, at the age of 11, without-- you see, if you don't live in a Jewish community, like the Jews in Poland, you-- you are part of the community. My parents were very respected in that community. The priest of the church was a friend of the family. And all our friends were non-Jew. My parents' friends were-- weren't Jewish. We were part of the community. [? As ?] [? a-- ?] then they would light the candles on Friday night. And there were holidays that were celebrated. But we were part of the community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Apart from that incident, were there any other anti-Semitic incidents during your school years at school number four?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, only once, a kid called me a Jew. So I waited when he went from school. We lived right next to the school. And having a general store, there were shovels and-- and things outside, as we have here. The-- the merchandise was outdoors. I waited for that kid. When he passed by, I took a shovel, hit him over the head. That's the last time somebody called me a Jew. Couldn't do this in Poland. But in my town, you could do it because the-- the parents would be in uproar that the child would-- would call me a name. But I wouldn't let it sit, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the language of instruction at the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish. And that's why I had problems because we spoke German at home. And I did speak outside in Silesian dialect. That's a language that is very different. It's a mixture of German and Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I went to school, I had to learn Polish. And I wasn't very good at it. However, I stood-- still did very well in school. I was the first in class and no trouble sailing through my entrance exam in to high school. What were your favorite subjects at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Greek mythology-- I loved it. And I took some French. I-- I hated Polish history because at home, I was so brainwashed that it's not my country, and it's not my history. But otherwise, I guess, in general, I-- my report card looked pretty good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were friendly with the other girls at the school? There were no problems in socializing as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- I had wonderful friends in school. There was no difference, whether in public school or high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you would visit one another's homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. And we-- I've some friends that one of particular was born the same day, and we would celebrate our birthdays together. I was invited to their homes to see the Christmas tree. And they knew we don't have Christmas, but they still invited me to come see the Christmas tree. We used to try-- we tried to pressure Mother into-- we also wanted, as all children, we also want Christmas tree. And we were told, in no uncertain terms, that this is not that-- what we do. And it was amazing that living in-- among non-Jews-- my mother did come from a very observant family. My grandfather, when he was alive, was going to shul every Saturday morning and Friday night. So she was brought up in a very religious home, kosher. And oh, it wasn't easy because there was no [NON-ENGLISH] and so on. But my grandmother was very observant. So I guess my mother brought it into the marriage with her. And the-- the pride in being Jewish was always there. We knew we were different. We were part of the community, but not altogether.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you finished your schooling there, where did you attend the gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Nowa Wies, in the neighboring town. The public school was right next door. But the high school was about 6 kilometers. so I walked to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you go with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'll-- I-- she didn't go to gymnasium. She finished her public school. It was only me. It was very expensive. You paid tuition. You wore a uniform. And that was Europe. That's not Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was not a cheaper position. You bought your books, all your-- even the pencil. You got nothing in school.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so that was a sacrifice for my parents, although they were doing well. We lived maybe much nicer than-- not maybe, for sure nicer-- than most of the people around us. But still, people did not spend money the way we do today. It was a different life. However, there was never a shortage of money to-- to spend it on interesting holidays. I started skiing at the age of 6. And in the school, I had a ski trip-- the high school-- my parents just let me go. It's no problem. If we went to another town-- a school trips, I was always included. My parents would never say that there's no money to go. So and we traveled all over because my father had the Harley-Davidson with a sidecar. And he put his whole family in it, my sister and I in the sidecar, my mother in the back. And we traveled to Breslau to see his grandparents and aunts. And we went to the mountains. In the summer mostly, once a week on Sunday, we would go to the mountains. And we would go climb the mountains. They weren't very high, 1,000 meters, which is 3,000 feet, approximately. And with nature and-- and parks and forests were very important in-- in our lives. And I think Father instilled that in us. And for that, I am grateful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the gymnasium, a bigger town, were there more Jewish students?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- not in my class. There was a synagogue. That's the synagogue we attended. And there were more Jewish families living there. But in my class, there was not-- I was again the only Jewish child. It didn't matter because I had all these friends. They were not-- not Jewish, but it didn't make a difference. I never encountered anti-Semitism like I hear from people that lived in Poland. We were part of the community.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were at school and you were looking towards the future, what were your dreams and your hopes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my dream was to learn to-- to become a doctor and go in medicine. It was something that women-- girls-- did not plan to do. But that was my-- my dream. I wanted to be a doctor. Of course, that dream was shattered when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Bielszowice, did you belong to any youth groups--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Jewish or otherwise?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no Jewish. There was nothing. But I belonged to the Girl Guides. Summer holidays, we spent quite often in Berlin with my aunt, who lived there. And so we would-- my mother would send us there. Some times we'd spent in the mountains. But I-- there was no-- there-- there were maybe Jewish youth organizations. But they were, again, much further, like 20 kilometers. And it was no way of getting there. There were very little buses. My father couldn't take us and leave his business. So there was really not that much to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your sister didn't go on to the gymnasium. What did she do when she finished her public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother always wanted to leave that town. And her desire to leave started in 1933 when the Nazis came to power. And she was determined to-- for us to learn something, a trade, that we could be useful if we emigrate, if we leave. And since my sister was not academically inclined, she tried first-- she wanted to work with flowers because of nature that we were introduced to. But gardening was not flowers in-- in that town. So that was not for her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then she went for-- to learn hairdressing. The family was in uproar. It was not something that a nice Jewish girl would do. But my mother was determined that she should have a trade, that wherever we go, that she could work, find work. You mentioned that your mother came from quite a religious Jewish background. How would you define the religious atmosphere in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well as I said, we observed all the holidays. Friday night was Shabbat. Candles were lit. It was Shabbat dinner. But living among non-Jews, the store was open on Saturday. You just did not close the store. But at least Friday night was-- was kept. And I think that was typical of German Jewish life. Passover-- it was very difficult to get matzah. You had to go to another town and bring everything. There was no refrigeration. So it-- it wasn't easy to get everything. However, the Seder, we used to have in our home. My grandmother wouldn't come because she didn't travel on Shabbat. And she was too old to walk. Hanukkah, I remember, was lighting the candles every night and then playing with the dreidel. And the thing you could win would be a nut or a candy. There were no gifts every night like here. We played the dreidel, we got a nut. And the Purim, my mother would take us to Hindenburg or to the neighboring town, Chorzow, where there were more Jewish people, for Purim parties. So she arranged it already with some relatives that lived there because she wanted us to know and get to feel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we would go to Hanukkah parties, Purim parties. And that was more or less the extent of our Jewish life in-- in that town. And she made sure that we learned Hebrew and that we went to get our education. That-- that what's- was her objective. So on a Friday-evening Shabbat, what food would you associate with Shabbat? What smells would I find in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we didn't know the-- the foods that they used in Poland. We had a German kitchen, like German food. So I don't think there was any-- like gefilte fish, I learned from my husband. Never saw it. We used to boil a carp, which I still love.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no special food. My mother would make hamantaschen for Purim. Then she would make kreplach for the different holidays. And of course, Passover-- we had Passover food. But otherwise, during the year, there was nothing specially Jewish about our food, not that I recall. What kind of books and newspapers did your parents have in the home? Were they of Jewish content?

speaker: interviewee
text: Goethe, Schiller, Heine, and German newspapers. I do not remember. Plus, as a child, I did not concern myself. The reason that I know about the German poets, because my mother loved to read poetry. And she would read it to us. And that's why I know so much German poetry, although I never read it. But when my mother read it to us, I would memorize it.
